content.text: Kubernetes as the GPU Orchestration Plane

Kubernetes is the de-facto standard for running containerised LLM workloads in production. Its declarative API, resource model, and ecosystem of operators make it possible to schedule GPU pods, enforce isolation between models, autoscale based on queue depth, and drain nodes safely for maintenance — all without bespoke scripts.
Kubernetes has no built-in knowledge of GPUs. The
is a DaemonSet that runs on every GPU node, registers GPU capacity with the kubelet, and handles device allocation when a pod requests

# Install via Helm (recommended)
helm repo add nvdp https://nvidia.github.io/k8s-device-plugin
helm repo update

helm upgrade --install nvdp nvdp/nvidia-device-plugin \
  --namespace kube-system \
  --set gfd.enabled=true \          # GPU Feature Discovery labels
  --set migStrategy=mixed \         # support MIG and full-GPU pods on same node
  --set deviceListStrategy=envvar    # inject NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES env var
After installation, each GPU node reports capacity in

Capacity:
  cpu:               96
  memory:            1055973Mi
  nvidia.com/gpu:    8          # 8 full GPUs
  pods:              110
Allocatable:
  nvidia.com/gpu:    8
The device plugin also exposes GPU Feature Discovery (GFD) labels that the scheduler can use for placement:

# Labels added by GPU Feature Discovery
kubectl get node gpu-node-01 --show-labels | tr ',' '\n' | grep nvidia

# Example output:
# nvidia.com/cuda.driver.major=535
# nvidia.com/cuda.runtime.major=12
# nvidia.com/gpu.memory=81920          # MB
# nvidia.com/gpu.product=NVIDIA-H100-SXM5-80GB
# nvidia.com/gpu.count=8

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: vllm-llama-7b
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: vllm-llama-7b
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: vllm-llama-7b
    spec:
      # Taint tolerance: GPU nodes carry nvidia.com/gpu=present:NoSchedule
      tolerations:
        - key: nvidia.com/gpu
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule

      # Prefer H100 nodes; fall back to A100
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            - weight: 100
              preference:
                matchExpressions:
                  - key: nvidia.com/gpu.product
                    operator: In
                    values:
                      - NVIDIA-H100-SXM5-80GB
                      - NVIDIA-H100-80GB-HBM3

      containers:
        - name: vllm
          image: vllm/vllm-openai:v0.4.3
          args:
            - --model=meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf
            - --tensor-parallel-size=1
            - --max-model-len=4096
            - --port=8000
          env:
            - name: HUGGING_FACE_HUB_TOKEN
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: hf-token
                  key: token
          resources:
            limits:
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
              memory: "24Gi"
              cpu: "8"
            requests:
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
              memory: "20Gi"
              cpu: "4"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          # Liveness probe: re-schedule pod if GPU process hangs
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 120
            periodSeconds: 30
            failureThreshold: 3
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 90
            periodSeconds: 10
For models requiring tensor parallelism across multiple GPUs, request multiple GPUs per pod. The NVIDIA Device Plugin allocates them from the same node automatically:

containers:
  - name: vllm-70b
    image: vllm/vllm-openai:v0.4.3
    args:
      - --model=meta-llama/Llama-2-70b-chat-hf
      - --tensor-parallel-size=4   # matches GPU count below
      - --max-model-len=8192
    resources:
      limits:
        nvidia.com/gpu: "4"     # 4 GPUs on the same node
        memory: "320Gi"
The scheduler guarantees all 4 GPUs land on a single node. vLLM's
must equal the GPU count so NCCL all-reduces stay intra-node and use NVLink bandwidth rather than Ethernet.
Node Taints and Dedicated GPU Pools
Without taints, the Kubernetes scheduler may place CPU workloads on expensive GPU nodes. Tainting GPU nodes reserves them exclusively for GPU pods:

# Taint all GPU nodes on cluster setup
kubectl taint nodes -l nvidia.com/gpu=present \
  nvidia.com/gpu=present:NoSchedule

# Alternatively, set via kubelet config at node join time (kubeadm):
# registerWithTaints:
#   - effect: NoSchedule
#     key: nvidia.com/gpu
#     value: "present"
GPU pods opt in with a matching
(shown in the pod spec above). Non-GPU workloads without the toleration are never scheduled to GPU nodes.

— hardware-enforced partitioning into isolated slices, each with dedicated memory bandwidth and SM capacity. This is the right tool when multiple small models or development workloads share a single physical GPU.

# Enable MIG on device 0 and create 7× 1g.10gb slices
nvidia-smi -i 0 -mig 1
nvidia-smi mig -cgi 1g.10gb,1g.10gb,1g.10gb,1g.10gb,1g.10gb,1g.10gb,1g.10gb -C

# Verify
nvidia-smi mig -lgi
# +-------------------------------------------------------+
# | GPU instances:                                        |
# | GPU   GI    CI    MIG Dev   Memory-Usage     SM  ECC  |
# |  0    1     0     0         0MiB /  9984MiB  14   0   |
# |  0    2     0     1         0MiB /  9984MiB  14   0   |
# ...

# Pod requesting a MIG slice
containers:
  - name: embedding-server
    resources:
      limits:
        nvidia.com/mig-1g.10gb: "1"   # single 10GB MIG slice
MIG requires supported hardware and persistent configuration. For development clusters on older GPUs or where MIG overhead is undesirable,
allows multiple pods to share a single GPU through context switching:
, a single physical GPU appears as 4 schedulable resources. Pods request
and share the device through time-slicing.
time-slicing has no memory isolation — a pod can OOM other pods on the same GPU. Use only for development, never for production serving.
The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) scales on CPU/memory metrics, which do not correlate with LLM request backlog.
(Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) scales on external metrics — request queue depth, Prometheus GPU utilisation, or Kafka lag:

# KEDA ScaledObject targeting vLLM deployment
# Scales based on pending request count in a Redis queue
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: vllm-scaledobject
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: vllm-llama-7b
  minReplicaCount: 1
  maxReplicaCount: 8
  cooldownPeriod: 300        # seconds before scale-down (GPU pods are expensive to restart)
  pollingInterval: 15
  triggers:
    - type: redis
      metadata:
        address: redis-service:6379
        listName: llm_request_queue
        listLength: "10"    # scale up when queue > 10 items per pod
    - type: prometheus
      metadata:
        serverAddress: http://prometheus:9090
        metricName: vllm_avg_generation_throughput
        threshold: "80"     # scale down when throughput < 80 tok/s
        query: |
          avg(vllm:avg_generation_throughput_toks_per_s{
            deployment="vllm-llama-7b"})
When pod autoscaling exhausts existing GPU node capacity, a node autoscaler must provision new GPU nodes.
(AWS-native) or the Cluster Autoscaler (cloud-agnostic) handles this:

# Karpenter NodeClass for GPU instances (AWS)
apiVersion: karpenter.k8s.aws/v1beta1
kind: EC2NodeClass
metadata:
  name: gpu-nodeclass
spec:
  amiFamily: AL2
  role: KarpenterNodeRole
  subnetSelectorTerms:
    - tags:
        karpenter.sh/discovery: my-cluster
  securityGroupSelectorTerms:
    - tags:
        karpenter.sh/discovery: my-cluster
  instanceStorePolicy: RAID0   # NVMe scratch for model caching
---
apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1beta1
kind: NodePool
metadata:
  name: gpu-nodepool
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        gpu-node: "true"
      taints:
        - key: nvidia.com/gpu
          value: "present"
          effect: NoSchedule
    spec:
      nodeClassRef:
        name: gpu-nodeclass
      requirements:
        - key: node.kubernetes.io/instance-type
          operator: In
          values: ["p4d.24xlarge", "p3.8xlarge", "g5.12xlarge"]
        - key: karpenter.sh/capacity-type
          operator: In
          values: ["spot", "on-demand"]
  limits:
    nvidia.com/gpu: 64       # cluster-wide GPU cap
  disruption:
    consolidationPolicy: WhenEmpty
    consolidateAfter: 30m    # reclaim empty GPU nodes quickly

Standard Kubernetes scheduling is pod-by-pod. A distributed training job needing 32 pods (one per GPU) can deadlock if only 28 GPUs are free: some pods start and hold GPUs while others remain Pending, wasting the claimed resources.
("all-or-nothing") addresses this: the scheduler only admits the full pod group simultaneously, or keeps all pending until capacity is available. Two projects implement this:
CRD, implements binpack/spread plugin. Widely used for PyTorch DDP and MPI jobs.
Extends Kubernetes scheduler with co-scheduling and priority preemption. Supports
gang semantics without replacing the scheduler.

# Volcano Job for 4-node distributed training (16 GPUs total)
apiVersion: batch.volcano.sh/v1alpha1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: llm-pretrain
spec:
  minAvailable: 4      # all 4 workers must schedule together
  schedulerName: volcano
  plugins:
    ssh: []
    svc: []
  tasks:
    - replicas: 4
      name: worker
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: trainer
              image: your-registry/llm-trainer:latest
              command: ["torchrun", "--nproc_per_node=4", "train.py"]
              resources:
                limits:
                  nvidia.com/gpu: "4"
              env:
                - name: NCCL_IB_DISABLE
                  value: "0"
                - name: NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL
                  value: "2"
          tolerations:
            - key: nvidia.com/gpu
              operator: Exists
              effect: NoSchedule
(Data Centre GPU Manager) runs as a DaemonSet and exposes GPU metrics to Prometheus. This is the standard production monitoring stack for GPU Kubernetes clusters.

# Install dcgm-exporter
helm repo add gpu-helm-charts https://nvidia.github.io/dcgm-exporter/helm-charts
helm upgrade --install dcgm-exporter gpu-helm-charts/dcgm-exporter \
  --namespace monitoring \
  --set serviceMonitor.enabled=true \   # creates Prometheus ServiceMonitor
  --set serviceMonitor.interval=15s

# Key metrics exposed (prefix: DCGM_FI_DEV_)
# DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL        — SM utilisation %
# DCGM_FI_DEV_MEM_COPY_UTIL  — memory bandwidth utilisation %
# DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED         — frame buffer (VRAM) used MB
# DCGM_FI_DEV_POWER_USAGE     — power draw Watts
# DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP        — temperature Celsius
# DCGM_FI_DEV_NVLINK_BANDWIDTH_TOTAL — NVLink bytes

# Grafana alert: GPU memory pressure
groups:
  - name: gpu_alerts
    rules:
      - alert: GPUMemoryHigh
        expr: |
          (DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED / DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_FREE) > 0.90
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "GPU {{ $labels.gpu }} on {{ $labels.node }} at >90% VRAM"

      - alert: GPUUtilLow
        expr: avg_over_time(DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL[30m]) < 20
        for: 30m
        labels:
          severity: info
        annotations:
          summary: "GPU idle >30min — check autoscaler scale-down"
Without quotas, a single team can exhaust all cluster GPUs. ResourceQuotas and LimitRanges enforce fair sharing:

# Namespace-level GPU quota (per team)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: team-gpu-quota
  namespace: team-alpha
spec:
  hard:
    requests.nvidia.com/gpu: "8"   # team-alpha may request up to 8 GPUs total
    limits.nvidia.com/gpu: "8"
    requests.memory: "500Gi"
---
# LimitRange: pods must always set GPU limits (no unlimited claims)
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: gpu-limits
  namespace: team-alpha
spec:
  limits:
    - type: Container
      max:
        nvidia.com/gpu: "4"    # no single container can claim >4 GPUs
      default:
        nvidia.com/gpu: "0"    # default is no GPU (explicit opt-in)
      defaultRequest:
        nvidia.com/gpu: "0"
Pod Disruption Budgets for Inference Stability

Node drains during upgrades or spot preemption would bring down all replicas of a deployment simultaneously without a PodDisruptionBudget. PDBs guarantee a minimum available serving capacity during disruptions:

apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: vllm-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 1     # at least 1 replica must remain up during node drain
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: vllm-llama-7b
, Kubernetes will drain nodes one at a time, waiting for the replacement pod to pass readiness before evicting the next. For latency-sensitive serving, set
GPU pods without the matching toleration stay Pending forever on tainted GPU nodes. Always include the taint toleration in pod templates.
— GPU resources are not overcommittable. Setting only requests without limits causes a validation error.
vLLM images are 10–20 GB. First scheduling on a node can take 15+ minutes for image pull. Pre-pull images with a DaemonSet or ECR pull-through cache.

Pods that download model weights from HuggingFace Hub on startup add 5–30 minutes before readiness. Use an init container or a ReadOnlyMany PersistentVolumeClaim pre-populated with model weights via a one-time Job.
Multi-GPU pods require accurate NCCL topology. Mount the host's
If a pod is OOM-killed by the kernel (CPU memory, not GPU VRAM), the GPU is released. But if the GPU process itself crashes, the device plugin may fail to reclaim it. The device plugin's
env var causes node-level plugin restart to recover stranded GPUs.

Kubernetes GPU orchestration requires the NVIDIA Device Plugin (GPU capacity reporting), explicit resource requests/limits in pod specs, taints/tolerations for pool isolation, and MIG or time-slicing for GPU sharing. KEDA handles request-driven autoscaling; Karpenter provisions GPU nodes on demand; Volcano adds gang scheduling for distributed training. DCGM Exporter feeds GPU metrics into Prometheus for alert-based scaling and operational visibility. Start with a tainted GPU node pool, a single vLLM deployment, and DCGM monitoring before adding KEDA and Karpenter.
